metadata.summary: Instrument APIs with Prometheus RED/USE metrics using bounded-cardinality labels, native histograms, and middleware. Covers metric naming, histogram bucket tuning, and cardinality anti-patterns.

content.title: API Metrics Collection: RED/USE with Prometheus
content.summary: Instrument every API using the RED method (Rate, Errors, Duration) per endpoint and USE method (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) per resource. Expose a /metrics endpoint via Prometheus client. Labels MUST be bounded: method, route template, status class only — never user_id, request_id, IP, or raw path.

why.p: Aggregate metrics without the RED/USE framework hide the most important failure modes: a slow endpoint buried by fast ones, a 4xx spike on one route, a saturated connection pool. The framework forces per-endpoint visibility and resource-level capacity tracking simultaneously.

Cardinality is the single biggest operational hazard with Prometheus. One metric with user_id as a label creates millions of time series and kills Prometheus within hours. Route templates (e.g., /users/:id) instead of raw paths, and status class (2xx/3xx) instead of exact codes, keep cardinality bounded for any realistic API.

list.item: Any API that needs production observability before or after launch.
Adding RED/USE dashboards before a planned launch or scale event.
Onboarding a new microservice into an existing Prometheus + Grafana stack.
Migrating from legacy APM SDKs to OpenTelemetry as the standard instrumentation layer.
Pre-product-fit prototypes — /health + log-to-stdout covers the minimal monitoring need without Prometheus setup overhead.
Internal tools used by fewer than 10 people daily — alert fatigue exceeds incident value.
Strict-latency embedded or industrial systems where Prometheus client allocations and OpenTelemetry SDK CPU usage break the SLO. Use sampled, async exporters or skip in-process collection.

rule.statement: Metric labels MUST be bounded: method (GET, POST, etc.), route template (/users/:id, NEVER /users/123), and status class (2xx, 3xx, 4xx, 5xx). Labels MUST NEVER include user_id, request_id, session_id, trace_id, email, IP address, or any high-cardinality identifier.
rule.rationale: High-cardinality labels create millions of time series. Prometheus stores each series in memory; a label like user_id on an API with 1M users creates 1M series per metric, consuming gigabytes of RAM and crashing Prometheus within hours. This is the single most common Prometheus production incident.

rule.statement: Histogram buckets MUST be tuned to the actual latency profile of the service, not left at Prometheus defaults (0.005s to 10s in 7 buckets). Use the observed p50/p95/p99 from logs to set buckets. On Prometheus 2.40+, prefer native histograms over fixed-bucket histograms for tail percentile accuracy.
rule.rationale: Default buckets with coarse resolution at the tail mean histogram_quantile(0.99, ...) returns values that are off by a factor of 2 or more. This causes SLO burn-rate alerts to fire late or not at all. Tuned buckets or native histograms give accurate p99 without guesswork.

rule.statement: Every service MUST expose a /metrics endpoint in Prometheus text format. The metrics middleware MUST run before authentication and response modification so every request is counted including rejected ones.
rule.rationale: Metrics missing rejected requests (401, 429) hide real load patterns and error rates. The scrape target must be reachable from the Prometheus server without authentication or with a separate scrape credential.

section.name: implementation
section.p: Wire a metrics middleware that records request count and latency for every request, with bounded labels.

example.title: Prometheus metrics middleware with bounded cardinality
example.code:
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, Gauge, generate_latest from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware import time
# Three core RED metrics with bounded label sets REQUEST_COUNT = Counter( "http_requests_total", "Total HTTP requests", ["method", "route", "status_class"] # route = template, status_class = 2xx/3xx/4xx/5xx )
REQUEST_LATENCY = Histogram( "http_request_duration_seconds", "HTTP request latency", ["method", "route"], # Tuned for an API with p99 target of 1s: buckets=[0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0] )
IN_FLIGHT = Gauge( "http_in_flight_requests", "Current in-flight requests" )
def status_class(status: int) -> str: return f"{status // 100}xx"
class MetricsMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware): async def dispatch(self, request, call_next): # Use route template from router, not raw path route = request.scope.get("route") route_path = route.path if route else request.url.path
IN_FLIGHT.inc() start_time = time.time() try: response = await call_next(request) latency = time.time() - start_time REQUEST_COUNT.labels( method=request.method, route=route_path, status_class=status_class(response.status_code) ).inc() REQUEST_LATENCY.labels( method=request.method, route=route_path ).observe(latency) return response finally: IN_FLIGHT.dec()
app.add_middleware(MetricsMiddleware)
@app.get("/metrics") async def metrics(): from fastapi.responses import Response return Response(generate_latest(), media_type="text/plain")
example.note: route_path uses the route template (/users/{id}) not the raw path (/users/123). status_class collapses 200/201/204 into 2xx, keeping label cardinality at 5 values instead of hundreds.

example.title: High-cardinality labels that kill Prometheus
example.code:
# DO NOT DO THIS — high-cardinality labels REQUEST_COUNT = Counter( "http_requests_total", "Total HTTP requests", ["method", "path", "status", "user_id"] # path = raw URL, user_id = unbounded )
# This creates 1M+ series for a 1M user API. Prometheus OOMs within hours. REQUEST_COUNT.labels( method="GET", path="/users/12345678", # raw path, not template status="200", # exact code, not class user_id="12345678" # unbounded cardinality ).inc()
example.note: Never use raw paths, exact status codes, or any user/request/session identifier as a Prometheus label. These belong in logs and traces, not metrics.

section.name: red-use-dashboards
section.p: Two dashboards, one consistent framework: RED per endpoint, USE per resource.

list.item: RED dashboard: per-endpoint rate (requests/s), error ratio (5xx/(total)), duration (p50/p95/p99 histogram_quantile). One graph row per endpoint family.
USE dashboard: per-resource utilization (CPU%, memory%, connection pool usage%), saturation (queue depth, connection wait time), errors (connection errors, timeouts).
Business dashboard: domain-specific counters (orders/min, payments/min, conversions). Wire custom counters in business logic, not middleware.

checklist.check: Implement request counter metric with method, route (template), status_class labels.
Implement request latency histogram with tuned buckets or native histogram.
Add custom business metrics counters (orders, payments, conversions).
Verify no high-cardinality labels (user_id, request_id, ip, raw path).
Expose /metrics endpoint in Prometheus text format.
Configure Prometheus scrape target and scraping interval (15s default).
Run cardinality lint script to catch label violations before deploy.
Create RED + USE dashboards in Grafana.
Load test monitoring overhead — middleware adds less than 1ms per request.

antipattern.description: Using user_id, request_id, or session_id as Prometheus metric labels.
antipattern.reason: Creates millions of time series. High-cardinality identifiers belong in logs and traces where per-event storage is expected, not in metrics where each unique label combination is a persistent time series consuming RAM.

antipattern.description: Using default Prometheus histogram buckets (0.005s to 10s) for a service with sub-100ms p99 target.
antipattern.reason: Default coarse buckets give inaccurate percentile estimates at the tail. histogram_quantile(0.99) returns values that can be off by 10x. Tune buckets to your latency distribution or use native histograms (Prometheus 2.40+).

antipattern.description: Disabling or skipping the metrics middleware for authenticated endpoints to avoid "overhead".
antipattern.reason: Missing metrics for authenticated routes hides 401/403 error rates and real traffic patterns on the most important endpoints. Middleware overhead is sub-millisecond and worth the full coverage.
